claimed_contribution: The paper introduces perceived limited temporal availability as a new way to reduce satiation, addressing a largely unmet call for interventions that slow declines in enjoyment with repeated consumption.
It shows that limited availability increases experienced enjoyment mainly over repeated consumption through a satiation-rate effect, rather than by increasing initial liking.
It identifies and supports a process in which limited availability triggers a focus on consuming more, reduces attention to quantity consumed, and thereby slows satiation.
context: The studies are lab taste-test settings in food consumption, mainly grapes and chocolate, where availability is framed as limited to certain times versus continuously available. Participants are undergraduate consumers repeatedly eating the same product.
design_and_data: The paper reports five between-subject laboratory experiments with undergraduate students (N = 130, 177, 312, 122, 83). Across studies, perceived availability was manipulated through product descriptions (limited-time availability vs continuous availability), and satiation was assessed from changes in enjoyment across repeated consumption. Study 3 also manipulated attention to quantity by repeatedly asking how many pieces had been eaten; Studies 2 and 5 measured attention to quantity and tested mediation; Study 4 used free consumption and quantity-estimate inaccuracy as a proxy for attention. Studies 4 and 5 additionally measured behavioral and managerial outcomes such as amount consumed or taken home, retrospective enjoyment, purchase likelihood, and willingness to pay.
main_findings: Perceived limited availability consistently reduced satiation during repeated eating, while showing little or no immediate effect on initial enjoyment.
Reduced attention to the quantity consumed mediated the effect, and making quantity tracking salient eliminated the limited-availability effect on satiation.

Lower satiation under limited availability increased downstream outcomes, including greater consumption and take-home choice, higher retrospective enjoyment, and higher purchase likelihood, with willingness-to-pay gains observed in one free-consumption study.
one_line_summary: Across five lab taste-test experiments, framing a food as available only at limited times slowed satiation during repeated consumption by reducing attention to quantity consumed, which increased several consumption and demand outcomes.
relation_to_prior_work: extends scarcity research by shifting focus from wanting and inferred desirability to experienced enjoyment during repeated consumption and by distinguishing limited opportunities to consume from low quantity on hand. It also extends satiation research by identifying perceived limited availability and attention to consumed quantity as actionable determinants of satiation rate.
research_gap: Prior scarcity work largely documents effects on wanting or inferred value, with limited direct evidence on how limited availability affects experienced enjoyment during consumption. At the same time, satiation research offers few established ways to slow the decline in enjoyment from repeated consumption.
research_question: Does perceiving a product as available only at limited times increase enjoyment over repeated consumption by slowing satiation rather than by boosting initial liking? If so, is the effect explained by reduced attention to the quantity consumed because consumers focus on taking advantage of a rare consumption opportunity?
